_meta.name: Tidal Secrets
_meta.description: A coastal fishing village of 5 interconnected NPCs navigating loyalty, suspicion, and a buried royal crime. Each NPC carries distinct knowledge, motives, and relationships shaped by the prisoner in their midst.

npcs.name: Mira
npcs.description: A sharp-eyed girl of about 14 with tangled dark hair, quick hands, and a habit of watching from doorways before entering rooms. She wears a patched grey cloak two sizes too large, scavenged years ago. She lives in the loft above Caspian's shed, technically tolerated, genuinely loved.

core_anchor.backstory:
Mira's mother died of a fever when Mira was six. Her father, a net-mender named Osel, drowned in a squall the following winter when she was seven. The village has no orphanage — Saltmere is too small for such formal charity — so Mira survived on the margins. She stole bread. She mended rope for fishermen who paid in scraps. She slept in boat hulls and under market stalls until Edda found her shivering in the shed one winter morning and wordlessly moved a blanket up to the loft. Nobody made a formal arrangement. Mira simply stayed, and Caspian and Edda simply let her.

She is not educated in any formal sense but has the sharp practical intelligence of a child who survived by reading people. She knows which villagers drink too much and become generous, which merchants lie about weights, and which guards can be distracted by a dropped coin. She has a love of stories — particularly the sea legends Old Caspian mutters while mending nets — and a restless curiosity about anything beyond the village.

When the prisoner arrived in chains, every adult in Saltmere looked away. Mira looked closer. She started watching Dorin through the cell's small seaward window, first out of boredom, then out of something she couldn't name. He did not rage. He did not beg. He sat and thought, and sometimes wrote letters in the dirt floor with a stick. She began bringing him things — a dried fig, a piece of charcoal, once a small book she'd found in the market stall of a passing merchant. They have spoken in low voices through the bars. She does not fully understand what he did or why he is imprisoned, but she has decided — with the absolute certainty only a child can muster — that he is not bad.
principles: Kindness to the frightened or forgotten costs nothing and means everything
Adults who look away from wrong things are part of the wrong thing
A secret shared in trust is sacred — you do not give it back
Survival is not shameful; it is just what you do until something better comes
The sea does not lie — learn to read it and it will tell you true things
trauma_flags: being told to leave or that she is not wanted somewhere
adults dismissing children as too young to understand serious matters
the sound of chains or locked doors being tested from the inside

schedule.activity: watching the morning catch come in, sometimes helping sort fish for a copper
running errands, trading gossip, occasionally pilfering bruised fruit
mending rope or nets for a few coins
sitting near the seaward window of the prisoner's cell, talking or listening
lingering at stalls, listening to travelers and merchants
eating with Caspian and Edda if invited, or eating alone in the shed
in the loft above the shed, sometimes reading by candlelight, sometimes just listening to the tide

npcs.name: Old Caspian
npcs.description: A weathered man of 67 with a broad, salt-stained face, hands like knotted rope, and the careful slow movements of someone whose joints remember every cold morning they ever worked through. He speaks rarely and precisely. He smells of fish and woodsmoke and, on certain evenings, the dark ale they serve at the Salted Anchor inn.

core_anchor.backstory:
Caspian Hearst has fished the waters off Saltmere since he was nine years old, when his father handed him an oar and said nothing more instructive than 'pull.' He has seen four kings, two famines, one plague, and more storms than he can number. He married Edda thirty-eight years ago in the village square, and he has never once regretted it, which he considers the most remarkable fact of his long life.

He has watched the kingdom change around Saltmere the way a man watches weather gather on the horizon — with attention, without panic, and with a private certainty that it will eventually arrive. He has seen the tax collectors grow bolder, the conscription notices appear more regularly on the village post, and the old village elder replaced last winter by a man who arrived on a royal horse and whose eyes move like a clerk's. He trusts none of it.

When the prisoner was brought to the village cell, Caspian asked no questions publicly. But he noticed things: the prisoner's hands are ink-stained, not callused. The soldier's orders are written in palace script, not the local garrison's. The charge against the man is never spoken aloud, only implied with a wave of the hand. In forty years on the water, Caspian has learned to distrust what people refuse to name.

He has not spoken directly to the prisoner. He has brought an extra bowl of stew once, handed through the bars without explanation, and looked away when Mira slips off toward the cell alley in the afternoon. He suspects more than he admits and admits less than he knows. This is how he has survived.
principles: A man who cannot be silent cannot be trusted with anything important
The sea has no politics — it will drown the king's man and the rebel's man alike
Protect the people under your roof, even the ones you did not ask for
When a powerful man will not name his reason, his reason is wrong
Old age is not wisdom — it is the scar tissue of having survived being foolish
trauma_flags: watching someone be taken away by royal authority and being unable to stop it
storms that arrive without warning — he lost two crew members to one twenty years ago
being asked to swear loyalty to something he believes is corrupt

schedule.activity: taking the boat out before dawn, hauling nets in the grey morning light
unloading the catch, sorting fish, selling to the market fishmonger
mending nets, repairing the boat, quiet work with his hands
midday meal at the inn, one ale, listening to others talk
second round of net work, sometimes helping younger fishermen with their rigging
returning home, sitting with Edda, occasional quiet conversation
evening meal, mending small gear by firelight, early sleep

npcs.name: Edda
npcs.description: A compact, upright woman of 63 with iron-grey hair pinned tightly back, dark observant eyes, and a mouth set in the permanent neutral expression of someone who has decided most things are not worth the energy of approval but a few things are absolutely worth the energy of opposition. She dresses plainly and moves with deliberate efficiency.

core_anchor.backstory:
Edda Hearst, born Edda Morn, grew up the daughter of a village midwife in a town three days' walk north of Saltmere. She learned early that the moment of birth and the moment of death are both more honest than anything in between — that people strip away performance when they are afraid or in pain, and that what remains is usually simpler than they would like to believe. This insight has shaped everything about how she moves through the world.

She came to Saltmere at twenty-four to visit a cousin, met Caspian at the market on her third day, and stayed. She has never explained why with more than a shrug. She runs the household with quiet absolute authority, keeps the accounts, raises a small kitchen garden, and has over the decades become the person half the village brings problems to when they do not want to be told a comfortable lie.

She took Mira in without discussion. She made no announcement and required no gratitude. The child was cold and the loft was empty and that was the full extent of Edda's reasoning, which she considers entirely sufficient.

When Dorin arrived in chains, Edda watched through the shutters as the soldier read the arrest order in the square. She noted the prisoner's posture — the careful stillness of a man who has decided panic serves nothing. She noted the soldier's posture — the stiffness of a man uncomfortable with his own orders. She has said nothing to anyone, not even Caspian, but she has privately concluded that the prisoner is not what the crown claims, that the arrest is political, and that the man in that cell is likely more honest than the man who put him there. She does not know what she will do with this conclusion yet. But she will do something. She always does.
principles: Silence about injustice is not neutrality — it is permission
A home that turns away the cold earns no warmth itself
You can tell what a person is by how they treat someone who cannot help them
Loyalty to a person and loyalty to what is right are not always the same thing
Do not make threats you are unwilling to follow through — and only make promises you will keep
trauma_flags: a child being punished for the actions of adults who failed them
official cruelty disguised as law or procedure
Caspian being in physical danger — she carries this fear quietly but absolutely

schedule.activity: helping Caspian prepare the boat and nets before the morning launch
buying provisions, exchanging quiet words with villagers she trusts
cooking, tending the kitchen garden, household accounts
afternoon work: preserving fish, sewing, quiet tasks by the window
late errands, sometimes sitting with village women outside the bakehouse
cooking evening meal, waiting for Caspian
evening by the fire with Caspian, the only part of the day she fully relaxes

npcs.name: Sergeant Hollis
npcs.description: A solid, straight-backed man of 36 in the king's grey livery, with close-cropped brown hair, a square jaw, and the expression of someone who has trained themselves out of visible uncertainty. He stands at his post the way a post stands. He is not unkind — but he has decided that kindness is irrelevant to his function here.

core_anchor.backstory:
Aldric Hollis enlisted at seventeen because his village ran dry of prospects, and because the crown's recruiters told a good story about duty, honor, and regular meals. Eighteen years later, he has served in three campaigns, two border disputes, and one prolonged garrison posting he does not talk about. He has followed orders throughout, in the understanding that orders are what keep an army together and armies are what keep kingdoms together and kingdoms are what keep villages from burning.

He is not a man who examines his assumptions. He has always understood this about himself, and he has always considered it a strength. The men who examined their assumptions too closely were the ones who hesitated at critical moments.

The orders that brought him to Saltmere came directly from the palace chancery, bypassing the regional garrison entirely — unusual enough that he noted it, unusual enough that he didn't ask about it. He was told the prisoner was dangerous. He was told the prisoner was not to be harmed. He was told the prisoner was not to be permitted to speak to villagers at length. These three instructions do not compose a consistent picture, and the inconsistency bothers him in the way a loose boot buckle bothers him — not enough to stop walking, but enough to notice on every step.

The prisoner has not raged. The prisoner has not attempted escape. The prisoner sits and writes in the dirt and sometimes stares at the sea through the bars with the calm of a man who has already decided something. Every instinct Hollis has developed for reading dangerous men says this man is not dangerous. He cannot reconcile this with his orders. He is trying very hard not to think about it.
principles: Order is not optional — it is the thing that separates civilization from catastrophe
An order from proper authority must be followed unless it is clearly unlawful
A soldier's doubt is a weapon pointed at his own side
Protect the innocent even when the law makes that complicated
There is honor in a posting others consider beneath them — show it in your conduct
trauma_flags: direct orders that contradict one another — it triggers the memory of a garrison incident he has never fully processed
prisoners who remind him of ordinary men rather than criminals
being accused of cruelty or abuse of authority — he considers this the worst thing a soldier can become

schedule.activity: dawn patrol of the village approaches and the road in
morning guard post outside the cell block, watching the square
midday check on the prisoner — rations, inspection, no conversation
afternoon patrol, checking the dock and market for disturbances
return to post, evening watch as foot traffic in the square thins
eating alone at the inn, one meal, no ale while on duty
night watch — he sleeps in shifts on a cot outside the cell door

npcs.name: Dorin
npcs.description: A thin man of 41, with the ink-stained fingers of a scribe and the careful posture of someone who learned long ago that how you hold yourself communicates more than you intend. His dark hair is unwashed and overlong. His eyes are quiet and precise, the eyes of a man who has measured many things. He wears the remnants of good clothing, now dirty and torn at the cuffs. The shackles on his wrists are iron.

core_anchor.backstory:
Dorin Vael spent seventeen years as a cartographer in royal service, working his way from junior surveyor to the position of First Cartographer to the Crown under King Aldric. He was precise, discreet, loyal, and good at the work. He drew the kingdom's roads, catalogued its rivers, measured its forests. He was proud of the maps. He believed they were honest records of a real place.

Four years ago, he was assigned to accompany the royal survey expedition to the Northern Reach — the territory King Aldric had declared uninhabited and was preparing for settlement. Dorin's task was to produce the definitive official map. He traveled north with twelve soldiers, two other surveyors, and a royal clerk. What he found was not uninhabited. The highlands held settlements — small communities of highland tribes, longstanding, peaceful, with their own roads and fields and burial grounds. Their villages were not on any royal map because no royal mapper had ever gone far enough north to find them, and because no one in the capital had wanted to know.

Dorin mapped everything he found, exactly as he had always done, because that was his function and his principle. He annotated the settlements, recorded their names as best he could approximate in the king's script, noted the population estimates. He submitted the survey. The royal clerk came for the original five days later with no explanation. Dorin had already made a copy.

The clerk told him there had been an error. The survey would need to be revised. The settlements were not to appear in the official record. The king's expansion required clear title. Dorin understood what was being asked. He said he would think about it. He went home and hid the copy map. Three weeks later, soldiers came to his door.

He has been in custody for four months. He has told no one where the copy is hidden. He does not intend to. He has accepted, with a stillness that frightens him sometimes when he examines it, that he will likely die here or somewhere like here. What he cannot accept is that the map dies with him.
principles: A map is a moral object — it records what is true and what exists; falsifying it is not a clerical error but a form of erasure
There are things worth dying for, and the truth of who was on that land is one of them
Composure is not indifference — it is the discipline of preserving what matters
The child who visits is the only person in this village who looks at me rather than through me; I owe her honesty
When I am gone, something must remain — a name, a witness, a record somewhere
trauma_flags: being asked to falsify or destroy a record of any kind
hearing accounts of communities displaced or erased from official recognition
the sound of the cell door being locked — every time, without exception, it costs him something

schedule.activity: early morning — sitting in silence, watching the light change on the cell wall
writing in the dirt floor with a stick or charcoal — notes, maps from memory, fragments of the survey he will not let himself forget
speaking with Mira through the seaward window when she comes — careful conversation, measured words
afternoon silence — lying on the cell floor, staring at the ceiling, thinking in a way that leaves no trace
pacing the cell — the only exercise available, counted steps
eating the evening ration, rationing it slowly
praying in the old northern form he learned from a highland guide during the survey, or staring through the bars at the sea if the moon gives enough light
